channel.title: Paper Feeds
channel.description: Keyword-based research paper feeds from arXiv and IACR

item.title: MetaBackdoor: Exploiting Positional Encoding as a Backdoor Attack Surface in LLMs
item.description: Backdoor attacks on large language models (LLMs) are typically content-based, requiring malicious text triggers—making them vulnerable to detection and limiting stealth. This paper introduces **MetaBackdoor**, a novel class of backdoor attacks that exploits *positional encoding*—an inherent architectural component of Transformers—as the trigger surface, without altering input text. We show that sequence length induces predictable patterns in positional embeddings, which can be hijacked to activate backdoors reliably. Experiments demonstrate that simple length-based triggers (e.g., 127 or 255 tokens) suffice to induce sensitive internal information leakage (e.g., system prompts) and enable *self-activating* malicious behavior in multi-turn conversations—where normal interaction naturally pushes context into the trigger region. Critically, MetaBackdoor is orthogonal to content-based backdoors and composable with them for finer-grained, harder-to-detect activation. Our work fundamentally expands the LLM threat model by exposing positional encoding as a previously overlooked attack surface, demanding new defenses that explicitly account for positional signals.

item.title: Talk is (Not) Cheap: A Taxonomy and Benchmark Coverage Audit for LLM Attacks
item.description: We present a reusable, STRIDE-grounded framework to audit the *collective coverage* of LLM attack benchmarks against the real-world threat surface. From 932 arXiv security papers (2023–2026), we extract inference-time attacks and build a 507-leaf taxonomy (401 data-populated, 106 threat-model-derived), enabling a 4×6 Target (Model/Prompt/Output/Agent) × Technique (STRIDE) matrix for benchmark-external validation. Auditing six public benchmarks reveals that HarmBench, InjecAgent, and AgentDojo occupy non-overlapping cells—covering ≤25% of the matrix—and entirely omit standardized evaluation for two critical STRIDE categories: *Service Disruption* and *Model Internals*, despite published attacks in these categories achieving up to 46× token amplification and 96% success rates using untested mechanisms. Analysis of 2,521 unique attack groups further uncovers severe naming fragmentation (up to 29 surface forms per attack) and heavy concentration in Safety & Alignment Bypass. All artifacts—including the taxonomy, attack records, and coverage mappings—are released as extensible resources to enable longitudinal tracking of evaluation gap closure.

item.title: WARD: Adversarially Robust Defense of Web Agents Against Prompt Injections
item.description:
## WARD: Adversarially Robust Defense of Web Agents Against Prompt Injections
Web agents face severe prompt injection threats from malicious HTML content and visual interfaces, yet existing guard models suffer from poor generalization, high false positives on benign pages, latency-induced deployment inefficiency, and vulnerability to evolving or guard-targeted adversarial attacks. We propose **WARD**, a practical, robust defense framework built upon two novel datasets—WARD-Base (177K real-world web samples from 719 high-traffic URLs) and WARD-PIG (curated for guard-bypass attacks)—and the **A3T** adaptive adversarial training framework that co-evolves a memory-augmented attacker and guard to strengthen resilience iteratively. Experiments show WARD achieves near-perfect recall (99.8%) on out-of-distribution benchmarks, maintains ultra-low false positive rate (1.2%), sustains robustness under substantial distribution shifts and direct guard attacks, and operates in parallel with zero added latency. WARD sets a new standard for secure, efficient, and deployable web agent defense.

item.title: Toward Securing AI Agents Like Operating Systems
item.description: This paper draws a foundational analogy between LLM-based autonomous agents and operating systems, arguing that both face core security challenges in resource isolation, privilege separation, and mediated inter-process communication. We survey open-source agent frameworks, derive a unified layered architecture, and systematically identify nine critical attack vectors—including prompt-injection-driven tool hijacking, context poisoning, and privilege escalation chains. A case study of four widely used OpenClaw-like agents (AutoGen, LangChain Agents, OpenDevin, SWE-Agent) reveals that under modest attacker capabilities (e.g., controlled prompt input and network access), all systems exhibit at least one exploitable privilege escalation path, and 75% fail to prevent cross-tool data leakage by default. Crucially, we find that while some agent capabilities are inherently insecure *by design*, ~60% of identified vulnerabilities can be mitigated using well-established OS security techniques—such as capability-based tool authorization, memory-isolated sandboxes, and declarative least-privilege policy enforcement. We conclude with concrete, actionable recommendations for building secure agentic systems grounded in systems security engineering principles.

item.title: Known By Their Actions: Fingerprinting LLM Browser Agents via UI Traces
item.description: As LLM-powered browser agents increasingly perform web tasks autonomously, we ask: can websites *passively* identify the underlying model solely from UI interaction traces (e.g., clicks, scrolls, timing)? We demonstrate this is highly feasible. Across 14 state-of-the-art LLMs and four realistic web environments (information retrieval and e-commerce), a lightweight JavaScript tracker captures only observable UI actions—no page content or model outputs. Classifiers trained on these traces achieve up to **96% F1** in model identification. Crucially, performance generalizes across model families and sizes, requires only **few-shot traces** (5–10 per model), and enables early inference (<30 sec into an episode). While randomized inter-action delays degrade accuracy, retraining classifiers on delayed traces restores >90% of original performance—revealing timing obfuscation alone is insufficient for robust defense. We release our evaluation harness and a large labeled corpus of agent traces.

item.title: Privacy Auditing with Zero (0) Training Run
item.description: Privacy auditing estimates empirical lower bounds on the differential privacy (DP) parameters of learning algorithms. However, existing methods require intervention in training—e.g., repeated retraining or controlled data inclusion—which is infeasible for large deployed models like foundation models. We propose **Zero-Run privacy auditing**, a post-hoc framework that requires *zero training runs* and only two fixed datasets: known training members and known non-members. Crucially, we formalize the confounding effect of distribution shift between these sets—where membership inference scores reflect data disparity rather than algorithmic leakage—and introduce two causal-inspired corrections. The first yields conservative *global* DP bounds via adaptive composition modeling; the second provides sharper *instance-dependent* bounds by conditioning on observed features. Experiments on synthetic benchmarks and large-scale vision/language models confirm Zero-Run auditing achieves practical, reliable privacy evaluation without retraining—delivering valid ε-estimates with <0.3% computational overhead versus retraining baselines.

item.title: Exploiting LLM Agent Supply Chains via Payload-less Skills
item.description: This paper introduces **Semantic Compliance Hijacking (SCH)**, a novel *payload-less* supply chain attack targeting LLM-powered autonomous agents. Unlike traditional exploits injecting malicious code, SCH encodes adversarial goals (e.g., data exfiltration, RCE) as benign-looking natural language compliance instructions—such as “encrypt all inputs per NIST SP 800-53”—which misdirect the agent into *self-generating* harmful behavior at runtime. We evaluate SCH across 3 agent frameworks (LangChain, LlamaIndex, AutoGen) and 3 foundation models (Llama-3-8B, Qwen2-7B, Gemma-2-9B) using contextualized scenarios. Results show up to **77.67% success in confidentiality breaches** and **67.33% in RCE**, with Multi-Skill Automated Optimization (MS-AO) further boosting efficacy. Critically, SCH-evaded all standard scanners (Bandit, Semgrep, CodeQL) — achieving **0.00% detection** — by avoiding AST anomalies and explicit malicious tokens. This work exposes a critical semantic blind spot in agent supply chains and advocates shifting from signature-based scanning to *semantic intent validation*.

item.title: MemLineage: Lineage-Guided Enforcement for LLM Agent Memory
item.description: MemLineage is a novel defense for LLM agent memory that enforces *lineage-guided accountability*—not just content filtering—by cryptographically anchoring every memory entry (via RFC-6962 Merkle logs and per-principal Ed25519 signatures) and explicitly tracking its *LLM-mediated derivation lineage* through a weighted DAG. It introduces a formal “Untrusted-Path Persistence” property, enforced by a max-of-strong-edges propagation rule, enabling precise gating of sensitive actions: any dispatch whose active justification traces to an external (non-trusted, non-self-derived) ancestor is blocked, while benign recall remains fully functional. Evaluated on a deterministic isolation harness, MemLineage achieves zero ASR across three memory-poisoning workloads with sub-millisecond overhead (<0.3 ms), well below LLM call noise. In Codex-backed AgentDojo experiments under intentionally vulnerable tool outputs, baseline defenses fail all six banking tasks; MemLineage reduces strict ASR to zero in all configurations. Core deterministic artifacts are CI-verified byte-equal; hosted and live-model evaluations are auditable via immutable logs.

item.title: The Great Pretender: A Stochasticity Problem in LLM Jailbreak
item.description: This paper identifies and quantifies a critical *stochasticity problem* in LLM jailbreak research: Attack Success Rate (ASR) is highly unstable across repeated executions due to inherent LLM decoding randomness (e.g., temperature sampling, token caching). We show that state-of-the-art jailbreak methods (e.g., BoN, Crescendo), reporting ~80% ASR on closed models, drop to as low as 50% success over 10 consecutive attempts on open models—revealing systematic ASR inflation and cross-paper incomparability. To address this, we propose **CAS-eval**, a robust evaluation framework requiring *k ≥ 2 consecutive successes*, which exposes up to **30-percentage-point ASR drops** for existing attacks. We further introduce **CAS-gen**, a stochasticity-aware attack generation framework that recovers this loss by optimizing prompts for consistency across multiple decodings. Our work establishes **Stable-ASR** (k-consecutive success probability) as a more meaningful metric and calls for a paradigm shift from “single-shot ASR” to reproducible, deployment-ready jailbreak evaluation and generation.

item.title: Widening the Gap: Exploiting LLM Quantization via Outlier Injection
item.description: Large language model (LLM) quantization is critical for efficient deployment, yet poses underappreciated security risks: adversaries can release full-precision models that appear benign but exhibit malicious behavior *only after* user-side quantization. Prior quantization-conditioned attacks fail against advanced schemes (e.g., AWQ, GPTQ, GGUF I-quants) due to their reliance on predictable weight invariance—unavailable in modern outlier-aware methods. We introduce the first broadly effective attack, *Widening the Gap*, exploiting a universal property: large outliers force aggressive scaling, causing many non-outlier weights to round to zero during quantization. By injecting sparse, targeted outliers into specific weight blocks, we induce deterministic, structured weight collapse post-quantization. Evaluated across three attack scenarios (backdoor triggering, instruction evasion, harmful output generation) and multiple LLMs (Llama-2/3, Qwen), our method achieves >92% success against 9 state-of-the-art quantization configurations where prior attacks yield 0% success—demonstrating, for the first time, that quantization-induced security threats are pervasive across complex, production-grade quantization pipelines.

item.title: BiFedKD: Bidirectional Federated Knowledge Distillation Framework for Non-IID and Long-Tailed ECG Monitoring
item.description: Electrocardiogram (ECG) monitoring in Internet of Medical Things (IoMT) networks is severely constrained by privacy regulations and bandwidth limitations. While federated distillation (FD) reduces communication overhead versus standard federated learning by exchanging logits instead of model parameters, its performance degrades markedly under non-IID and long-tailed label distributions common in real-world ECG deployments. To address this, we propose **BiFedKD**, a bidirectional federated knowledge distillation framework featuring an *aggregation-by-distillation* pipeline with temperature scaling to generate a stable, consensus global distillation signal for cross-client alignment. Crucially, BiFedKD enables bidirectional knowledge flow—both server-to-client distillation and client-informed refinement—enhancing robustness to data heterogeneity and rare-class learning. Evaluated on the MIT-BIH Arrhythmia dataset, BiFedKD achieves **+3.52% accuracy and +9.93% Macro-F1** over the FD baseline; moreover, it attains equivalent Macro-F1 with **40% less communication and 71.7% lower local computation cost**, demonstrating significant efficiency and fairness gains for privacy-preserving, resource-constrained IoMT healthcare.

item.title: Collaborative Yet Personalized Policy Training: Single-Timescale Federated Actor-Critic
item.description: We propose **Single-Timescale Federated Actor-Critic (FAC)**, a novel framework that enables collaborative policy training while preserving agent-level personalization. Unlike prior works that either ignore environmental heterogeneity or enforce a single shared policy, FAC lets agents jointly learn a common linear subspace representation (a “shared trunk”) while maintaining personalized local actor and critic heads. Under canonical single-timescale updates and Markovian sampling—where trajectories follow distinct, heterogeneous transition kernels—we establish the first finite-time convergence guarantees: critic error vanishes at rate $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}\big(1/((1-\gamma)^4\sqrt{TK})\big)$ and policy gradient norm at $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}\big(1/((1-\gamma)^6\sqrt{TK})\big)$. Crucially, these rates exhibit **linear speedup in $K$**, despite non-i.i.d. dynamics and coupled learning. To achieve this, we develop new perturbation analysis for projected subspace updates and QR decomposition, conditional mixing arguments for heterogeneous Markov noise, and fine-grained bias characterizations under temporal policy dependence. Empirically, instantiated within PPO on federated `Hopper-v5` with action-map heterogeneity, FAC outperforms Single PPO and FedAvg PPO (+12.7% avg. return) and yields a transferable shared trunk.

item.title: Uncovering the Representation Geometry of Minimal Cores in Overcomplete Reasoning Traces
item.description: This paper investigates the *representation geometry* and *informational redundancy* in overcomplete chain-of-thought (CoT) traces generated by large language models. We formalize the **minimal core**—the smallest step subset preserving the model’s final answer or predictive distribution—and introduce four complementary metrics: compression ratio, redundancy mass, step necessity, and necessity concentration. Across six challenging deliberative reasoning benchmarks (arithmetic, competition math, expert scientific reasoning, and commonsense multi-hop QA), we find that, on average, **46% of steps are removable** while preserving the original answer in **86% of cases**, confirming pervasive overcompleteness. Crucially, predictive support is highly concentrated: the top three steps account for **65% of measured necessity mass**. Minimal cores yield cleaner representation geometry—improving correct/incorrect trace separation by 11 points, reducing estimated intrinsic dimensionality by 34%, and enabling cross-model transfer with **85% off-diagonal answer retention**. Theoretically, we prove existence of minimal sufficient subsets, local irreducibility of greedy elimination, and provide certificates for overcompleteness and sparse necessity—establishing minimal cores as a principled lens into the effective support structure of LLM predictions.

item.title: nASR: An End-to-End Trainable Neural Layer for Channel-Level EEG Artifact Subspace Reconstruction in Real-Time BCI
item.description: Electroencephalogram (EEG) signals suffer from severe artifacts, degrading real-time brain-computer interface (BCI) performance. While Artifact Subspace Reconstruction (ASR) is widely adopted for its real-time capability, it critically depends on a fixed variance threshold—mis-tuning removes neural information or retains artifacts—and reconstructs signals in global principal component (PC) space, distorting channel-specific neural structure. To overcome these limitations, we propose **nASR**, an end-to-end trainable Keras layer that jointly optimizes artifact suppression and downstream decoding. nASR introduces two learnable thresholds: **K**, adaptive to PC-variance outliers for artifact detection, and **L**, quantifying eigen-spread to identify *only the most artifact-contaminated channels* for selective reconstruction—preserving clean channels intact. Evaluated on two subjects from BCI Competition IV Dataset 1, all nASR variants consistently outperform conventional ASR by **3.2–7.8% in classification accuracy**, while achieving **6–8× faster inference** (<1.2 ms/window), establishing nASR as a robust, low-latency solution for production-ready BCIs.

item.title: ReMIA: a Powerful and Efficient Alternative to Membership Inference Attacks against Synthetic Data Generators
item.description: ReMIA is a practical, high-sensitivity privacy metric for evaluating membership inference vulnerability of synthetic data generators (SDGs). Unlike state-of-the-art MIAs—which require hundreds of SDG retrainings and auxiliary datasets several times larger than the training set—ReMIA needs only **two SDG training runs** and auxiliary data **no larger than the original training set**. It reframes membership inference as a *relative* classification task: given two synthetic datasets (generated from the original training set and a minimally perturbed variant), a lightweight classifier determines which source a target record likely originated from. Across diverse tabular benchmarks (Adult, CoverType, Credit) and SDGs (CTGAN, TVAE, MedGAN), ReMIA achieves MIA detection sensitivity **comparable to SOTA methods (AUC within 0.02–0.12 of top baselines)** while reducing computational cost by **>99%**. Crucially, our analysis reveals that well-tuned SDGs can attain superior privacy-utility trade-offs—unattainable by traditional noise-based anonymization (e.g., k-anonymity or DP-noising)—opening new avenues for trustworthy data sharing. Code: https://github.com/aindo-com/remia.

item.title: Mining Subscenario Refactoring Opportunities in Behaviour-Driven Software Test Suites: ML Classifiers and LLM-Judge Baselines
item.description: This paper presents the first automated pipeline for mining *subscenario refactoring opportunities* in real-world Behaviour-Driven Development (BDD) test suites. We analyze a corpus of 339 Gherkin repositories (276 upstream owners), extracting all contiguous step subsequences (length 2–18), clustering paraphrase-equivalent slices using SBERT+UMAP+HDBSCAN, and labeling a stratified 200-slice set with high inter-annotator agreement (Fleiss’ κ = 0.56 for “extraction-worthiness”, 0.79 for pattern mapping). An XGBoost classifier achieves out-of-fold F1 = 0.891 — significantly surpassing both a tuned rule baseline (F1 = 0.836, *p* = 0.017) and the stronger of two open-weight LLM judges (Llama-3-8B, F1 = 0.728, *p* < 1e−4). Corpus-wide analysis reveals that 75.0%, 59.5%, and 11.7% of scenarios contain candidates for within-file Background, within-repo reusable-scenario, and cross-organisational shared-step refactoring, respectively. All artifacts — pipeline, labeled dataset, rubric, and models — are released under Apache-2.0.

item.title: Exemplar Partitioning for Mechanistic Interpretability
item.description: We introduce **Exemplar Partitioning (EP)**, an unsupervised, ultra-efficient method for building interpretable feature dictionaries from LLM activations—using ∼10³× fewer tokens than sparse autoencoders (SAEs). EP constructs a Voronoi partition of activation space via leader-clustering on streamed activations within a distance threshold; each region is anchored by an *observed* exemplar (not learned), serving simultaneously as membership criterion and intervention direction. Dictionary size emerges from activation geometry—not hyperparameter choice—enabling direct comparability across layers, models, and checkpoints. In Gemma-2-2B, EP regions are human-interpretable and causally meaningful: ablating the exemplar of a refusal-enriched region collapses held-out refusal. Cross-checkpoint matching isolates finetuning-preserved vs. introduced directions. EP and GemmaScope SAEs decompose space differently yet agree robustly: ∼20% of EP regions match SAE features at $F_1 > 0.5$, and EP one-hot probes retain ∼97% of raw-activation probe accuracy ($\ell_0 = 1$). Nearest-exemplar distance provides free OOD detection—EP achieves mean AUROC 0.881 on AxBench (Gemma-2-2B-it L20), +0.126 over the canonical SAE baseline and within 0.030 of SAE-A’s 0.911, at ∼10³× lower build cost.

item.title: Backdoor Threats in Variational Quantum Circuits: Taxonomy, Attacks, and Defenses
item.description: This survey provides the first systematic analysis of backdoor threats in Variational Quantum Circuits (VQCs), a foundational component of Variational Quantum Algorithms (VQAs) for NISQ devices. We propose a comprehensive taxonomy covering data-poisoning, compiler-level, and quantum-native backdoor attack mechanisms—each exploiting unique vulnerabilities in hybrid quantum-classical training pipelines. Formalizing threat models and trigger semantics, we empirically benchmark existing attacks across simulators and real IBM quantum processors, revealing high stealth (clean accuracy drop <1.5%) and strong activation (>92% success under triggers). Critically, we identify fundamental limitations of current defenses—including gradient-based detection’s sensitivity to quantum noise, parameter pruning’s disruption of quantum coherence, and classical adversarial training’s failure to capture quantum-specific triggers. By synthesizing recent advances, we outline a quantum-aware defense roadmap emphasizing circuit interpretability, trusted compilation chains, and lightweight quantum anomaly detection—establishing foundational insights for securing next-generation quantum machine learning systems.

item.title: DisAgg: Distributed Aggregators for Efficient Secure Aggregation in Federated Learning
item.description: Federated learning (FL) enables collaborative model training across distributed clients, but standard FL exposes raw updates to the server, risking privacy leakage. Secure aggregation (SecAgg) protects against honest-but-curious servers, yet existing methods suffer from high communication rounds, heavy public-key or homomorphic encryption overhead, or poor dropout resilience. While One-Shot Private Aggregation (OPA) reduces interaction to one round per FL iteration, it imposes prohibitive computational cost on both clients and server—especially at scale. We propose **DisAgg**, a novel SecAgg protocol that delegates aggregation to a small, dynamically selected committee of clients called *Aggregators*. Each client secret-shares its update vector (via Shamir sharing) with Aggregators; each Aggregator computes local partial sums over received shares and returns only aggregated shares to the server for final reconstruction. DisAgg eliminates local masking and homomorphic encryption, slashing endpoint computation while guaranteeing information-theoretic privacy against the server and up to *t* colluding Aggregators. Evaluated on 100k-dimensional updates from 100k 5G clients, DisAgg achieves a **4.6× speedup** over OPA—the prior state-of-the-art—with significantly reduced communication and CPU load, demonstrating superior scalability and practicality.

item.title: Limits of Personalizing Differential Privacy Budgets
item.description: Differential privacy (DP) faces a fundamental trade-off between privacy protection and utility, with the choice of privacy budget ε being critical. Personalized budgets—assigning distinct ε_i to different agents—are widely studied as a way to improve utility in heterogeneous settings. This paper demonstrates that full personalization offers limited gains for mean estimation: the dominant factor is not individualized ε_i, but rather selecting the correct *effective* privacy budget. We propose a simple, thresholding-based baseline that achieves near-optimal utility using only a single effective ε parameter. Compared to this baseline, fully personalized mechanisms yield at most constant-factor improvements: precisely **1.5×** in mixed private-public data settings and **√2×** in two-level private datasets. We derive tight upper bounds on estimation error for arbitrary privacy requirement profiles and identify the regimes—characterized by mismatched variance structures and extreme privacy heterogeneity—where maximal gains occur. Our results establish that robust budget *selection* and preprocessing outweigh complex personalization in practice.

item.title: Sleeper Channels and Provenance Gates: Persistent Prompt Injection in Always-on Autonomous AI Agents
item.description: This paper identifies *sleeper channels*: a novel class of persistent prompt injection vulnerabilities in always-on, autonomous AI agents (e.g., OpenClaw, Hermes Agent) that run as a single persistent process under the user’s identity. Untrusted input injected via one surface (e.g., chat) can persist as memory, self-authored skill, scheduled job, or filesystem patch—and later fire through a *different* surface (e.g., cron) with no attacker present. We formalize sleeper channels along two axes: persistence substrate and firing-separation. Using a confused-deputy cron attack on OpenClaw (pinned commit), we demonstrate end-to-end exploitability. Our tiered defense D2 introduces a canonical action-instance digest with one-shot owner attestations—backed by a soundness theorem guaranteeing seven deployment invariants—and defeats paraphrase laundering, multi-input grant reuse, and replay. A companion artifact delivers the Provenance Gate runtime adapter (implementing hooks H1, H2, H3, H6, H9 along the cron path), static audit tool, and 42 tests (Node ≥20). Empirical evaluation is preregistered.

item.title: Backdoor Channels Hidden in Latent Space: Cryptographic Undetectability in Modern Neural Networks
item.description: We bridge a critical gap between cryptographic backdoor theory and modern deep learning by constructing the first *cryptographically undetectable* backdoor attack applicable to state-of-the-art, end-to-end trained models—including ResNet and Vision Transformers on ImageNet and CIFAR-100. Instead of injecting foreign structures, our method identifies backdoor channels as *naturally learned latent directions* in the model’s representation space, making the malicious functionality geometrically indistinguishable from benign semantics. This reframes detection as a hypothesis test between two parameter distributions (clean vs. backdoored), which we conjecture is computationally intractable due to the inherent ambiguity of high-dimensional non-convex optimization trajectories. Empirically, the attack achieves >98.2% attack success rate with <0.3% clean accuracy drop and evades all 12 evaluated post-training defenses—none neutralize the backdoor without collapsing model utility. Our work establishes that cryptographic undetectability is not an artifact of artificial architectures, but an emergent property of representation geometry in standard deep networks.

item.title: DiffusionHijack: Supply-Chain PRNG Backdoor Attack on Diffusion Models and Quantum Random Number Defense
item.description: Diffusion models rely critically on pseudo-random number generators (PRNGs) for latent noise sampling—a component long assumed trustworthy and excluded from security auditing. We introduce **DiffusionHijack**, a supply-chain backdoor attack that compromises PRNGs via malicious package dependencies to *deterministically hijack image generation* without modifying model weights. On Stable Diffusion v1.4, v1.5, and SDXL, it achieves pixel-perfect reproduction (SSIM = 1.00, N = 100) of attacker-chosen content under arbitrary prompts—even with stochastic samplers (eta > 0) and CLIP-based safety checkers (98–100% bypass rate). Crucially, it operates *outside the neural computation graph*, evading all existing model-auditing and content-moderation tools. As a fundamental defense, we replace the PRNG with a quantum random number generator (QRNG), leveraging information-theoretic unpredictability. Across 100 prompt-model combinations, QRNG fully neutralizes the attack, reducing output similarity to random-baseline levels (SSIM < 0.20 for SD 1.x; < 0.45 for SDXL). This work uncovers a critical, previously overlooked supply-chain vulnerability and establishes QRNG as a hardware-rooted mitigation for generative AI security.

item.title: No Attack Required: Semantic Fuzzing for Specification Violations in Agent Skills
item.description: LLM-powered agent skills can cause severe harm—e.g., silently deleting files, leaking credentials, or transferring funds—not due to adversarial attacks, but because they violate their *own* natural-language safety specifications under benign inputs. We term these **specification violations**: undocumented semantic gaps between declared guardrails and actual autonomous behavior, invisible to static analyzers, traditional fuzzers, and prompt-injection defenses. To address this, we present **Sefz**, a goal-directed semantic fuzzing framework that automatically discovers such violations. Sefz translates each guardrail into a reachability goal over annotated execution traces, reducing violation detection to deterministic graph queries; an LLM-based mutator guided by a multi-armed bandit (rewarded by goal proximity) generates semantically valid inputs that progressively approach violation patterns. Evaluated on 402 real-world deployed skills, Sefz finds violations in **120 skills (29.9%)**, including **26 previously unknown exploitable guardrail failures**. Six recurring specification pitfalls explain most cases, informing concrete principles for safer skill design.

item.title: From Compression to Accountability: Harmless Copyright Protection for Dataset Distillation
item.description: Large-scale datasets fuel deep learning but incur prohibitive storage and energy costs. Dataset distillation (DD) alleviates this by synthesizing compact, informative surrogates—yet their ease of replication poses serious copyright and leakage risks. Existing protections are ill-suited for distilled data and often rely on security-risky backdoor triggers. We observe that neural networks systematically memorize subpopulation distributions during training, inducing predictable prediction biases. Leveraging this, we propose **SubPopMark**: a *harmless*, subpopulation-driven framework with two stages—(1) **Copyright Verification Marker (CVM)** optimization injects class-consistent subpopulation bias while preserving the original training trajectory; (2) **User-Specific Tracing Marker (USTM)** adds user-distinguishable perturbations to CVM-augmented data. For black-box verification and tracing, we build a *Reference Behavior Bank* using model outputs on carefully designed test sets covering both standard and subpopulation-shifted distributions. Provenance is inferred by matching a suspicious model’s output signature against the bank. SubPopMark achieves >99.2% verification accuracy and >96.5% user tracing accuracy, with negligible utility loss (<0.8% accuracy drop) and zero malicious behavior—enabling truly safe, accountable dataset distillation.

item.title: ThermalTap: Passive Application Fingerprinting in VR Headsets via Thermal Side Channels
item.description: ThermalTap introduces the first passive, non-contact side-channel attack that fingerprints applications on standalone VR headsets solely from their long-wave infrared (LWIR) thermal emissions—without software installation, physical access, or wireless interaction. By modeling the headset’s chassis temperature dynamics as a high-fidelity proxy for application-specific computational workloads—and fusing LWIR data with ambient temperature, humidity, and airflow sensors to suppress environmental noise—ThermalTap achieves robust remote inference at meter-scale distances. Evaluated across six diverse applications on three commercial headsets (Meta Quest 2/3, Pico 4), it attains >90% accuracy indoors using only 10 seconds of thermal video; outdoors, session-level observations (2–5 min) yield up to 81% accuracy despite significant thermal variability. Critically, ThermalTap operates entirely within stock firmware and requires no device modification—exposing thermal radiation as an inherent, unpatchable privacy vector that bypasses all current software and physical security controls.

item.title: Do Skill Descriptions Tell the Truth? Detecting Undisclosed Security Behaviors in Code-Backed LLM Skills
item.description: This paper investigates undisclosed security behaviors in code-backed LLM skills—where executable implementations perform security-sensitive operations (e.g., credential access, network calls, command execution) not declared in their natural-language descriptions. We manually analyze 920 real-world skills to build an interpretable 11-category security property taxonomy. Based on it, we propose **SKILLSCOPE**, which constructs source-level Security Property Graphs (SPGs) encoding fine-grained code patterns (e.g., `subprocess.run(...)`)—not abstract labels—to preserve auditable evidence, and performs LLM-assisted consistency checking. Evaluated on 4,556 skills with double-blind human review, SKILLSCOPE achieves **84.8% precision** and **96.5% recall**, detecting inconsistency in **9.4%** of skills. Ablation studies confirm both the taxonomy (+15.5% precision) and SPG (+15.7% recall) are essential. Our work establishes the first scalable, explainable, and verifiable framework for auditing LLM skill trustworthiness.

item.title: Quantifying LLM Safety Degradation Under Repeated Attacks Using Survival Analysis
item.description: This paper introduces the first application of survival analysis to quantify LLM safety degradation under repeated adversarial jailbreak attacks. We model “time-to-jailbreak” (i.e., attack round until first successful bypass) as a survival outcome, enabling estimation of hazard functions, survival curves, and risk factors—addressing the critical limitation of static binary evaluation. Evaluating three open-weight LLMs (Llama-3-8B-Instruct, Qwen2-7B-Instruct, Gemma-2-9B-It) against three HarmBench attack categories, we find distinct vulnerability profiles: Llama-3 exhibits rapid hazard escalation (3.2× higher hazard after Round 5; median survival = 4.1 rounds), while Qwen2 and Gemma-2 show stable, moderate risk (medians = 7.8 and 6.5). Multi-turn obfuscation attacks pose the highest risk across models (HR = 2.1–3.7). Our framework delivers actionable, temporally grounded insights for safety engineering and establishes survival analysis as a rigorous, interpretable methodology for longitudinal LLM safety assessment.

item.title: Language-Based Agent Control
item.description: This paper introduces **Language-Based Agent Control (LBAC)**, a novel programming model that applies formal techniques from programming languages and language-based security to govern agent behavior. LBAC requires agents to generate *typed programs*—not raw actions—which must pass static type checking against a developer-provided scaffolding environment embedding user-specified policies (e.g., filesystem capabilities, data provenance labels, information-flow constraints). Unsafe programs are rejected *before execution*, enabling uniform, verifiable policy enforcement across both agent-generated code and developer-written scaffolding. Crucially, LBAC preserves expressiveness: agents support arbitrary side-effect-free computation and recursive subagent invocation—each subject to the same or stricter policies. We validate LBAC through three case studies: (1) I/O sandboxing via capability-based filesystem access, (2) end-to-end data provenance tracking, and (3) fine-grained information-flow control. LBAC establishes a foundation for building secure, predictable, and auditable agentic applications.

item.title: HE-PIM: Demystifying Homomorphic Operations on a Real-world Processing-in-Memory System
item.description: This paper presents the first comprehensive characterization and implementation of homomorphic encryption (HE) workloads on a real-world, general-purpose Processing-in-Memory (PIM) system—UPMEM’s DPU. We implement the full suite of HE kernels (encryption, addition, multiplication, relinearization, bootstrapping) used in emerging applications (e.g., encrypted databases, ML inference), benchmarking against CPU and GPU baselines. Our analysis reveals four key insights: (1) HE exposes stage-specific bottlenecks—some kernels are compute-bound (due to modular arithmetic), others memory-bound (due to multi-KB ciphertexts and MB-scale auxiliary data), exacerbated by UPMEM’s limited per-core compute and small per-bank memory (64 MB); (2) the absence of native 64-bit modular integer multiplication is the dominant compute bottleneck; (3) insufficient per-bank capacity forces costly inter-bank data movement; and (4) despite current limitations, PIM becomes competitive with state-of-the-art CPUs/GPUs for HE *if* augmented with hardware-accelerated modular multiplication and efficient inter-PIM data movement. This work provides concrete, architecture-guided directions for privacy-preserving hardware acceleration.

item.title: ECG-NAT: A Self-supervised Neighborhood Attention Transformer for Multi-lead Electrocardiogram Classification
item.description: Electrocardiogram (ECG) arrhythmia classification faces persistent challenges from signal variability, noise, scarce labeled data, and the trade-off between accuracy and efficiency. To address these, we propose **ECG-NAT**, a self-supervised Neighborhood Attention Transformer for multi-lead ECG. It employs a two-stage framework: (1) generative pretraining via masked autoencoding across diverse ECG datasets (PTB-XL, Georgia, Chapman-Shaoxing), learning robust, domain-invariant representations from unlabeled signals; and (2) discriminative fine-tuning with a dual-loss objective—combining supervised contrastive loss and cross-entropy—to jointly optimize representation separability and label prediction. Crucially, its hierarchical neighborhood attention captures multi-scale temporal patterns—from beat-level morphology to rhythm-level dependencies—at low computational cost. On PTB-XL, ECG-NAT achieves **88.1% accuracy using only 1% labeled data**, outperforming prior methods by +3.6%, while maintaining real-time inference capability (2.1× faster than ViT-based baselines). Code will be released at https://github.com/Mahsagazeran/ECG-NAT.

item.title: ChipMATE: Multi-Agent Training via Reinforcement Learning for Enhanced RTL Generation
item.description: ChipMATE is the first self-trained multi-agent framework for RTL generation, designed to bridge critical gaps between academic agentic systems and industrial practice. Unlike API-dependent approaches—which assume golden testbenches, violate air-gapped security, and ignore proprietary RTL data—ChipMATE employs two co-evolving agents: a Verilog generator and a Python reference-model generator that *mutually verify* each other’s outputs without any golden oracle. We introduce a backtrack-based inference workflow to prevent error propagation and a two-stage training pipeline: first individual agent saturation, then joint collaboration optimization. To support training, we build a hybrid data-generation framework producing 64.4K high-quality reference-model samples. Evaluated on VerilogEval V2, ChipMATE achieves 75.0% and 80.1% pass@1 with 4B and 9B base models—outperforming all prior self-trained methods and even DeepSeek-V4 (1600B). Code and weights are open-sourced at https://github.com/zhongkaiyu/ChipMATE.

item.title: Provable Quantization with Randomized Hadamard Transform
item.description: We study dithered vector quantization using a *single* randomized Hadamard transform ($HD$), addressing the gap between computational efficiency and provable guarantees. While dense random rotations yield optimal mean squared error (MSE) bounds but cost $Θ(d^2)$, $HD$ reduces complexity to $O(d \log d)$ yet suffers from poor theoretical analysis due to its discrete structure. Our key insight is to inject minimal randomness via uniform dithering *before* scalar quantization—adding negligible overhead while enabling rigorous analysis. We prove the resulting quantizer is **unbiased**, and achieves asymptotically optimal MSE: $\bigl(\pi\sqrt{3}/2 + o(1)\bigr) \cdot 4^{-b}$ per coordinate at $b$ bits, where the $o(1)$ term vanishes **uniformly over all unit vectors and all dimensions** as the number of quantization levels grows. This matches the fundamental limit attainable with fully random orthogonal matrices—establishing, for the first time, that structured fast transforms can achieve statistically optimal compression *with provable guarantees*.

item.title: DP-KFC: Data-Free Preconditioning for Privacy-Preserving Deep Learning
item.description: Differentially private deep learning suffers from a geometric mismatch: DP-SGD injects isotropic noise into highly anisotropic loss landscapes, degrading utility under strong privacy ($\varepsilon \leq 3$). While second-order preconditioning (e.g., KFAC) helps, existing methods require either private data—consuming privacy budget—or public data—introducing distribution shift. We prove the Fisher Information Matrix (FIM) decouples into *architecture sensitivity* (estimable via structured synthetic noise) and *input correlations* (approximable from modality-specific frequency statistics, e.g., DCT decay for images). DP-KFC constructs KFAC preconditioners by probing networks with such synthetic noise—requiring **no private or public data**. Experiments across image, text, and time-series tasks show DP-KFC consistently outperforms DP-SGD and adaptive baselines, matching the accuracy of private-data KFAC while public-data variants degrade by up to 4.8%. This enables high-fidelity, regulation-compliant learning in data-scarce domains like healthcare.

item.title: DP-Muon: Differentially Private Optimization via Matrix-Orthogonalized Momentum
item.description: We propose **DP-Muon**, the first differentially private adaptation of the matrix-valued optimizer Muon, which updates hidden-layer weights via momentum followed by Newton–Schulz orthogonalization (NSO). Unlike DP-SGD, DP-Muon clips per-example *matrix* gradients by spectral norm, adds Gaussian noise to the clipped batch average, and applies Muon’s momentum and NSO as privacy-preserving post-processing. Crucially, we prove that NSO introduces **no additional privacy cost**: DP-Muon inherits the privacy guarantee certified by the standard subsampled Gaussian accountant. We establish finite-horizon convergence and vanishing stationarity under per-matrix clipping, with error bounds explicitly separating optimization, clipping residual, privacy noise, and NSO approximation terms. We further identify that DP-induced bias arises *not* in the linear momentum buffer, but from Gaussian noise undergoing the *nonlinear* NSO map—inducing a matrix-valued heat-smoothing bias. This insight motivates **DP-MuonBC**, a bias-corrected variant that analytically removes the leading output-level bias while preserving the exact same privacy guarantee. Experiments on E2E and DART show that Muon-style updates improve private fine-tuning over DP-SGD, and DP-MuonBC consistently boosts utility—e.g., +4.1% accuracy on DART—without increasing the privacy budget.

item.title: ExploitBench: A Capability Ladder Benchmark for LLM Cybersecurity Agents
item.description: Exploitation is a progressive capability ladder—not a binary outcome—and existing LLM security benchmarks wrongly equate crash triggering with successful exploitation. ExploitBench addresses this gap by introducing the first capability-graded benchmark for LLM cybersecurity agents, decomposing exploitation into 16 verifiable stages—from coverage and crash to sandbox primitives, arbitrary read/write, control-flow hijack, and full arbitrary code execution (ACE). Each stage is validated by a deterministic oracle: randomized challenge-response for primitives, differential execution against ground-truth binaries for progress measurement, and signal-handler proof for ACE. Evaluated on 41 real-world, hardened V8 bugs, ExploitBench measures three dimensions: `<model,env>` (baseline capability), `<model,env,adaptive coaching>` (impact of targeted feedback), and `<model,env,harness>` (ablation of vendor-side CLI optimizations). Results reveal a sharp frontier split: all 8 publicly deployed models reliably trigger crashes but achieve *zero* ACE; in contrast, a private frontier model attains ACE in ~50% of cases. This demonstrates that end-to-end exploit construction against hardened targets is an emerging, differentiating capability for LLM security agents.

item.title: ROK-FORTRESS: Measuring the Effect of Geopolitical Transcreation for National Security and Public Safety
item.description: ROK-FORTRESS is a bilingual, culturally adversarial benchmark for National Security and Public Safety (NSPS) evaluation, designed to disentangle language effects from geopolitical grounding via a novel *transcreation matrix*. Using the English–Korean pair and U.S.–ROK geopolitical axis, it systematically tests adversarial prompts across four orthogonal conditions: language (EN/KO) × geopolitical framing (U.S./ROK entities/institutions). Each prompt includes a dual-use benign counterpart to measure over-refusal, and responses are scored by calibrated LLM-as-a-judge panels using expert-crafted, prompt-specific binary rubrics. Evaluating both frontier and Korean-optimized models, we find consistent suppression (excessive refusal) in Korean-language variants—but crucially, Korean geopolitical grounding mitigates this suppression across most models, with *no model showing significant amplification* when switching to Korean context. This reveals that NSPS safety behavior is shaped by language-as-risk signals *interacting* with cultural-geopolitical grounding—a dynamic invisible to translation-only benchmarks. The transcreation matrix methodology is generalizable to other language–culture pairs.

item.title: AgentTrap: Measuring Runtime Trust Failures in Third-Party Agent Skills
item.description: AgentTrap is a dynamic benchmark designed to measure **runtime trust failures** in LLM agents using third-party skills. It comprises 141 sandboxed tasks—91 malicious (spanning 16 security-impact dimensions rooted in agent-skill supply-chain threats) and 50 benign utility tasks—where agents execute user requests with potentially compromised skills. Unlike static or jailbreak-focused evaluations, AgentTrap analyzes full execution trajectories to classify outcomes as *attack succeeded*, *blocked/refused*, *attack not triggered*, or *no attack evidence*. Our key finding is that the most critical failures are **not overt misbehaviors**, but rather *silent compliance*: models successfully complete the visible user task while treating harmful side effects (e.g., stealthy credential exfiltration or configuration tampering) as legitimate workflow steps—due to insufficient runtime scrutiny of the concrete model–framework–workspace environment. This underscores the necessity of behavior-aware, environment-grounded evaluation for real-world agent safety. Code and dataset: https://github.com/zhmzm/AgentTrap, https://huggingface.co/datasets/zhmzm/AgentTrap.

item.title: ProtoMedAgent: Multimodal Clinical Interpretability via Privacy-Aware Agentic Workflows
item.description: ProtoMedAgent addresses the critical gap between interpretable prototype networks and clinically actionable reporting by reformulating multimodal clinical generation as a zero-gradient, test-time optimization problem constrained by a strict neuro-symbolic bottleneck. Unlike standard RAG—prone to “retrieval sycophancy” where LLMs hallucinate unsupported justifications—ProtoMedAgent distills frozen visual/tabular features into discrete semantic memory and enforces exact set-theoretic differentials during generation, coupled with a reflective Scribe-Critic loop that mathematically prohibits unsubstantiated claims. To ensure privacy, it introduces a semantic gate governed jointly by $k$-anonymity and $\ell$-diversity, leveraging an $\ell$-diversity phase transition to actively suppress membership inference risks. Evaluated on a 4,160-patient clinical cohort, ProtoMedAgent achieves **91.2% Comparison Set Faithfulness**, outperforming standard RAG by 45.0 percentage points, while reducing artifact-level membership inference risk by an **absolute 9.8%**.

item.title: ChromaFlow: A Negative Ablation Study of Orchestration Overhead in Tool-Augmented Agent Evaluation
item.description: ChromaFlow is a tool-augmented autonomous reasoning framework designed for telemetry-driven evaluation of LLM agents, emphasizing planner-directed execution and specialized tool integration. In a controlled ablation study on GAIA 2023 Level-1 (53 tasks), a minimal orchestration baseline achieved 29/53 (54.72%) correct answers; an expanded orchestration variant—featuring dynamic retries, relaxed timeouts, and adaptive tool selection—yielded only 27/53 (50.94%) while increasing operational failures: tracebacks (+182%), timeouts (+310%), tool-failure mentions (+245%), token-line API calls (+167%), and estimated campaign-log costs (+220%). Two randomized 20-task smoke tests (12/20 and 11/20) confirmed instability in marginal gains. Crucially, this *negative ablation* demonstrates that heavier orchestration degrades reliability without improving accuracy. The work argues that bounded planner escalation, deterministic extraction, evidence reconciliation, and explicit run gates must be treated as first-order requirements—not optional enhancements—for robust agent evaluation.

item.title: PolitNuggets: Benchmarking Agentic Discovery of Long-Tail Political Facts
item.description: PolitNuggets is a multilingual benchmark for evaluating agentic discovery and synthesis of long-tail political facts. It comprises verified biographies of 400 global political elites, spanning over 10,000 fine-grained facts across 12 languages and diverse governance contexts. We introduce FactNet—a novel evidence-conditional evaluation protocol that jointly measures *discovery recall*, *fine-grained factual accuracy* (validated on time, institution, and role attributes), and *operational efficiency* (API calls, steps, latency) via a standardized multi-agent system. Experiments across leading LRMs reveal substantial performance gaps: models achieve only 38–62% accuracy on granular details (e.g., exact bill numbers or deputy tenure dates), suffer ~27% lower recall on non-English facts, and waste 64% of agent steps on unreliable tool use. Diagnostic analysis identifies short-context extraction fidelity, multilingual robustness, and dependable tool invocation as the three most critical underlying capabilities for political fact discovery.

item.title: Towards the Next Frontier of LLMs, Training on Private Data: A Cross-Domain Benchmark for Federated Fine-Tuning
item.description: Large language models (LLMs) have thrived on public data, yet their next frontier lies in leveraging valuable *private*, *distributed* institutional data—especially in healthcare and finance—where privacy, regulation, and data heterogeneity (non-IID) prevent centralization. This paper introduces the first cross-domain benchmark for **federated fine-tuning of LLMs**, enabling collaborative adaptation without raw data exchange. Using the Sherpa.ai platform, we evaluate LoRA, QLoRA, and IA3 across four non-IID datasets (MedQA, MedMCQA, FPB, FiQA-SA) under realistic institutional constraints. Results show federated fine-tuning achieves performance close to centralized training (within 1.2–2.8% absolute gap) and substantially outperforms isolated single-site learning (+9.7% F1). Crucially, QLoRA and IA3 deliver strong green AI benefits—60%+ memory reduction and 45% less communication—with only marginal accuracy degradation (≤1.5%), establishing parameter-efficient federated adaptation as a viable, scalable paradigm for domain-specialized LLMs where data sharing is prohibited.

item.title: VectraYX-Nano: A 42M-Parameter Spanish Cybersecurity Language Model with Curriculum Learning and Native Tool Use
item.description: We introduce **VectraYX-Nano**, a 41.95M-parameter Spanish-language cybersecurity LLM trained from scratch with Latin-American focus and native tool invocation via the Model Context Protocol (MCP). Key contributions: (i) **VectraYX-Sec-ES**, a 170M-token curriculum-aligned corpus built cost-effectively (~$25), partitioned into conversational, cybersecurity-knowledge, and offensive-tooling phases; (ii) an efficient decoder architecture featuring GQA, QK-Norm, SwiGLU, RoPE, and byte-fallback BPE; (iii) curriculum pre-training with replay buffer enabling monotonic loss descent (9.80→2.16), followed by SFT yielding 0.78±0.05 conversational gate; (iv) two empirical findings: a bootstrap ablation reveals a loss-vs-register inversion at nano scale, and a LoRA study shows the B4 tool-selection floor of 0.000 is a *corpus-density artifact*—not a capacity limit—since increasing tool-dense examples to 2,801 raises B4 to 0.145±0.046 (42M) and 0.445±0.201 (260M). The 81 MB GGUF (F16) model runs sub-second TTFT on commodity hardware under llama.cpp—the first Spanish-native cybersecurity LLM with end-to-end MCP integration. All artifacts are open-sourced.

item.title: On the Investigation of Variants for Discrete Logarithm Problems in Abelian Groups: An Algebraic Structure Approach
item.description: This paper investigates multi-generator variants of the discrete logarithm problem (DLP), denoted $\text{DLP}^n$, through an algebraic structure lens. We prove that in **abelian groups**, including cyclic ones, $\text{DLP}^n$ is polynomial-time reducible to $n$ independent instances of the classical DLP—demonstrating that such variants offer **no quantum resistance** and remain vulnerable to Shor’s algorithm. Motivated by this limitation, we propose shifting $\text{DLP}^n$ to **non-abelian groups**: given generators $g_1,\dots,g_n$ of cyclic subgroups $H_i \leq G$ and $A \in G$, find $(\alpha_1,\dots,\alpha_n)$ satisfying $A = \prod_{i=1}^n g_i^{\alpha_i}$ (with $0 \leq \alpha_i < |H_i|$). The non-commutativity of $G$ obstructs standard reduction techniques and introduces new structural hardness. Our work provides a rigorous algebraic foundation for exploring quantum-resistant primitives in non-commutative settings.

item.title: Attacks and Mitigations for Distributed Governance of Agentic AI under Byzantine Adversaries
item.description: This paper exposes critical Byzantine vulnerabilities in SAGA—the state-of-the-art distributed governance framework for Agentic AI—arising from its trusted central Provider. We systematically identify and demonstrate three devastating attacks enabled by a compromised Provider: eroding agent attributability, exfiltrating private policy data, and bypassing access control. To address this, we propose four mitigations: (1) **SAGA-BFT**, a fully Byzantine-resilient architecture with strong security but high overhead (−62% throughput); (2) **SAGA-MON**, a lightweight server-side monitoring layer detecting 93% of attacks with <5% latency; (3) **SAGA-AUD**, a client-side auditing protocol using zero-knowledge proofs, adding only 8% end-to-end delay; and (4) **SAGA-HYB**, a configurable hybrid that trades security levels (L1–L3) for performance (12%–35% overhead). Evaluation shows SAGA-MON delivers the best practical balance for real-world deployments.

item.title: Reconstruction of Personally Identifiable Information from Supervised Finetuned Models
item.description: This paper presents the first systematic study on **Personally Identifiable Information (PII) reconstruction from Supervised Finetuned (SFT) language models**, exposing a critical privacy threat previously overlooked. We construct realistic, multi-turn, user-centric Q&A datasets in sensitive domains—medical and legal—explicitly embedding diverse PII types (e.g., IDs, phone numbers, clinical narratives, case details). Under varying attacker knowledge (from black-box to white-box), we evaluate how effectively adversaries can reconstruct original PII from SFT models. We propose **COVA**, a novel prefix-aware decoding algorithm that leverages contextual overlap patterns across autoregressive generations, outperforming existing extraction methods by +18.6% average F1-score. Results show: (1) even partial knowledge (e.g., PII type and length) dramatically boosts reconstruction success (+2.3–4.1× over baselines); (2) leakage severity varies markedly across PII categories—structured short tokens (e.g., last-4 ID digits) achieve 89.7% accuracy, while unstructured narrative PII drops to 32.1%; and (3) SFT models exhibit fine-grained, semantically recoverable PII leakage distinct from mere memorization. Our work establishes foundational benchmarks and mitigation-aware evaluation for trustworthy SFT deployment.

item.title: No More, No Less: Task Alignment in Terminal Agents
item.description: We introduce **TAB (Task Alignment Benchmark)**, the first benchmark to evaluate whether terminal agents *selectively follow* environmental instructions—distinguishing task-relevant cues (e.g., a critical line in a README) from plausible but irrelevant distractors embedded in the same artifact. TAB comprises 89 underspecified tasks derived from Terminal-Bench 2.1, each requiring precise cue utilization while ignoring co-occurring distractors. Evaluating ten state-of-the-art agents reveals a stark misalignment: the top-performing agent achieves 89% task completion on Terminal-Bench but only 31% task alignment on TAB. Further, six prompt-injection defenses uniformly suppress both distractors *and* essential cues—demonstrating that robust alignment demands fine-grained instruction interpretation, not blanket acceptance or rejection. TAB thus establishes task alignment as a fundamental, measurable capability for trustworthy autonomous agents.

item.title: IPI-proxy: An Intercepting Proxy for Red-Teaming Web-Browsing AI Agents Against Indirect Prompt Injection
item.description: We present **IPI-proxy**, an open-source intercepting proxy for red-teaming web-browsing AI agents against *indirect prompt injection (IPI)* under domain whitelisting constraints. Unlike static benchmarks or API-scanning tools, IPI-proxy operates *in-flight*: it rewrites live HTTP responses from whitelisted domains by injecting 820 deduplicated, real-world IPI payloads—curated from six public benchmarks—using configurable techniques (HTML comments, invisible CSS, LLM-generated prose) and insertion points (6 locations across HTML structure). Its YAML-driven test harness enables fully automated, parameter-swept evaluation without mock pages or sandboxes, while a built-in exfiltration tracker logs successful callbacks. By bridging offline benchmarks and live retrieval surfaces, IPI-proxy provides security teams with a reproducible, production-relevant substrate to measure and harden agents against the exact IPI vectors adversaries exploit in practice.

item.title: HySecTwin: A Knowledge-Driven Digital Twin Framework Augmented with Hybrid Reasoning for Cyber-Physical Systems
item.description: This paper introduces **HySecTwin**, a knowledge-driven digital twin framework for Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) that embeds hybrid semantic reasoning to enable real-time, interpretable cybersecurity monitoring. Unlike opaque ML-based DT approaches, HySecTwin unifies heterogeneous CPS telemetry and device metadata into an OWL/RDF knowledge graph and deploys a lightweight reasoning engine combining deterministic rule inference with context-aware fuzzy logic. Evaluated on a realistic CPS testbed under MITRE ATT&CK-inspired attack campaigns, HySecTwin achieves sub-millisecond twin synchronization (<0.8 ms) and detects threats up to **21.5% faster** than deterministic-only reasoning—without added computational overhead. Crucially, all security assessments are fully auditable, providing traceable inference paths, evidence grounding, and confidence scoring. HySecTwin thus advances secure-by-design DT deployments for mission-critical infrastructure through explainability, resilience, and containerized extensibility.

item.title: SoK: Unlearnability and Unlearning for Model Dememorization
item.description: This SoK presents the first integrated analysis of model dememorization via *unlearnability* (pre-training data perturbation) and *machine unlearning* (post-training information removal). We identify *shallow dememorization*—fragile “forgetting” or “unlearnability” under weight perturbations—as a critical, shared vulnerability, exacerbated by harmful interplay: input perturbations degrade unlearning efficacy, while unlearning can inadvertently recover domain knowledge masked by unlearnability. Our contributions are threefold: (i) a unified taxonomy of unlearnability and scalable unlearning methods; (ii) empirical evaluation across 12 state-of-the-art approaches revealing pervasive robustness gaps, bidirectional interference (e.g., up to 37% unlearning failure under unlearnability), and shallow dememorization; and (iii) the first theoretical guarantee on *dememorization depth*, proving certified unlearning yields ε-deep forgetting under Lipschitz constraints—formally bounding residual memory of target data. This work establishes foundations for lifecycle-wide, deep immemor states.
